description:
You are acting as a deployment engineer who has just received a refreshed
  Python dependency specification for an internal application.  
  In the project workspace two requirement-files already exist:

    • /home/user/app/requirements_old.txt   (the versions currently in use)  
    • /home/user/app/requirements_new.txt   (the newly approved versions)

  Your objectives are as follows (perform the steps in any order you like, but
  **all** must be completed exactly as stated):

  1. Create the directory  
        /home/user/update_logs  
     if it does not already exist.

  2. Compare the package names and versions that appear in
        requirements_old.txt
     with the ones that appear in
        requirements_new.txt.

  3. Generate the report file  
        /home/user/update_logs/update_report.csv  
     with **precisely** the following CSV format (including header row,
     lower-case package names, no extra spaces):

        package,version_before,version_after,status
        <pkg-1>,<old-ver-1>,<new-ver-1>,<status-1>
        <pkg-2>,<old-ver-2>,<new-ver-2>,<status-2>
        …

     a. “status” must be the string  
        • upgraded  – if the versions differ (newer in the _new_ file)  
        • unchanged – if the versions are identical  
        (For this particular task every listed package is expected to be
        “upgraded”, but your script/commands must still be able to handle the
        “unchanged” case in general.)

     b. The CSV rows (after the header) must be ordered **alphabetically by
        package name** so that automated checking can rely on deterministic
        line positions.

     c. The file must end with a single newline character (no blank lines
        after the last data row).

  4. Display the finished CSV report to the terminal so that a human reviewer
     can see it immediately after creation.

  5. Do not hard-code the version numbers in your commands or script; read the
     two requirements files programmatically or with suitable shell utilities.

  The automated test suite will check only:

    • existence of /home/user/update_logs/update_report.csv  
    • exact byte-for-byte contents of that file  
    • that the file’s permissions allow regular read access (0600 or 0644 are
      both acceptable)  

  Nothing else will be graded, but you are encouraged to keep your command
  history concise and to write comments in any script you create.

  NOTE: You do not need root privileges, internet access, or to install any
  additional packages.  Standard GNU/Linux command-line tools present in a
  minimal distribution (e.g., awk, sed, sort, cut, paste, diff, cat, echo)
  are sufficient.
truth:
Initial file tree and file contents provided to the agent:

  1. /home/user/app/requirements_old.txt  
     ───────────────────────────────────  
     requests==2.25.0  
     numpy==1.18.0  
     pandas==1.1.0  
     ───────────────────────────────────  

  2. /home/user/app/requirements_new.txt  
     ───────────────────────────────────  
     requests==2.31.0  
     numpy==1.24.0  
     pandas==2.0.3  
     ───────────────────────────────────  

  Expected final artefact created by the agent:

  • /home/user/update_logs/update_report.csv  
    (permissions: 0644)

    Exact required file content:

    package,version_before,version_after,status
    numpy,1.18.0,1.24.0,upgraded
    pandas,1.1.0,2.0.3,upgraded
    requests,2.25.0,2.31.0,upgraded

    The file must terminate with a single newline character (i.e., there is a
    ‘\n’ byte after the final “upgraded”).

  No additional files, directories, or processes are mandatory for the test,
  but the agent may create temporary working files in /home/user as long as
  they do not interfere with the above verification.
